Text: Knot theory is a branch of topology investigating mathematical knots, distinguished from everyday knots by their closed, intertwined structure lacking loose ends. These mathematical knots are projections of three-dimensional embeddings of circles within three-dimensional space, classified by their topological equivalence – meaning they can be continuously deformed into one another without cutting or gluing. Invariants, such as the Jones polynomial and knot group, are utilized to differentiate knots; knots sharing the same invariants are considered equivalent, though possessing identical invariants doesn’t guarantee equivalence. Applications extend beyond pure mathematics, finding relevance in fields like molecular biology, particularly in understanding DNA and protein structures, and physics, specifically in string theory and quantum field theory.

Text: Parthenogenesis is a form of asexual reproduction in which an egg develops into an individual without being fertilized by a male gamete, resulting in offspring genetically identical to the maternal parent, often observed in lower eukaryotic organisms such as certain species of aphids, wasps, and fish, as well as in some invertebrates like rotifers and nematode worms, where it may serve as a reproductive strategy to ensure population survival in environments with scarce males or to facilitate colonization of new habitats, although its frequency and occurrence are relatively rare in higher eukaryotes including mammals and birds.

Typically, because of the difficulty to isolate atoms in order to measure their radii separately, the atomic radius is measured in a chemically bonded state; however, theoretical calculations are simpler when considering atoms in isolation.

Text: Electronegativity represents the atomic property quantifying the tendency of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons within a chemical bond. Differences in electronegativity between bonded atoms dictate bond polarity, ranging from nonpolar covalent bonds (minimal difference) to polar covalent bonds (intermediate difference) and ultimately ionic bonds (substantial difference). Electronegativity increases across a period from left to right and decreases down a group in the periodic table, reflecting increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius, respectively, influencing electron attraction.

Text: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory is a fundamental model in chemistry used to predict the geometry of molecules based on minimizing repulsion between electron pairs surrounding a central atom. These electron pairs, comprising both bonding and non-bonding (lone) pairs, arrange themselves to maximize spatial separation, dictating the molecular shape. The number of electron domains—areas of high electron density—around the central atom determines the electron-pair geometry, while the influence of lone pairs results in distortions from ideal geometries, leading to diverse molecular shapes like linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, and octahedral. VSEPR theory provides a relatively simple, yet powerful, method for understanding and rationalizing molecular structures and their associated properties.

Text: Active transport moves substances across membranes against their concentration gradients, requiring energy input. Symporters move both substances in the same direction, antiporters in opposite directions. Glucose absorption in intestinal epithelial cells is driven by a sodium-glucose symporter. Vesicular transport moves large molecules and particles through endocytosis and exocytosis, involving membrane fusion and fission. Active transport is fundamental to nutrient absorption, waste excretion, and maintaining cellular ion homeostasis.

Text: Cladistics ( klə-DIST-iks; from Ancient Greek κλάδος kládos 'branch') is an approach to biological classification in which organisms are categorized in groups ("clades") based on hypotheses of most recent common ancestry. However, from an empirical perspective, common ancestors are inferences based on a cladistic hypothesis of relationships of taxa whose character states can be observed. Theoretically, a last common ancestor and all its descendants constitute a (minimal) clade.

Text: Acoustic emission testing detects high-frequency stress waves generated by rapid energy release events within materials including crack initiation, propagation, fiber breakage, and corrosion, providing real-time passive monitoring of structural integrity without requiring controlled excitation of the component under test. Felicity ratio measurements of Kaiser effect violation during proof testing reveal accumulated damage extending material behavior beyond previously experienced loading. Text: Junctions between two or more nanotubes have been widely discussed theoretically. Such junctions are quite frequently observed in samples prepared by arc discharge as well as by chemical vapor deposition. The electronic properties of such junctions were first considered theoretically by Lambin et al., who pointed out that a connection between a metallic tube and a semiconducting one would represent a nanoscale heterojunction. Such a junction could therefore form a component of a nanotube-based electronic circuit. The adjacent image shows a junction between two multiwalled nanotubes.
Label: Human

Text: The taiga, also known as the boreal forest, is a vast and ecologically diverse biome characterized by coniferous tree species that dominate the landscape. Covering approximately 17% of the Earth's land area, the taiga spans across subarctic and temperate regions, primarily in North America and Eurasia. The boreal forest typically features a closed canopy of spruce, fir, and pine trees, with a dense understory of shrubs and herbaceous plants. The cold, subarctic climate and short growing season have led to the evolution of specialized plant and animal species that are well adapted to this harsh environment, including the iconic Arctic fox and the mighty grizzly bear.
Label: Meta

Text: In mathematics, combinations refer to the selection of distinct elements from a larger set where the order of selection does not matter. The number of possible combinations is determined using the binomial coefficient, denoted as C(n, k) or "n choose k," which is calculated by dividing the factorial of the total number of elements (n!) by the product of the factorial of the number of elements chosen (k!) and the factorial of the difference between the total elements and chosen elements ((n-k)!). Combinations are widely used in probability theory, statistics, and various fields of science to analyze scenarios where the arrangement of selected items is irrelevant, distinguishing them from permutations, where order is significant.

Text: Hypertension, commonly known as high blood pressure, is a chronic medical condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure, which increases the risk of cardiovascular diseases, stroke, and kidney failure. It is typically classified into primary (essential) hypertension, with no identifiable cause, and secondary hypertension, resulting from underlying conditions such as renal disease or endocrine disorders. Risk factors include genetic predisposition, obesity, high sodium intake, physical inactivity, excessive alcohol consumption, and stress. Diagnosis is based on repeated blood pressure measurements, with values consistently exceeding 140/90 mmHg considered hypertensive. Management involves lifestyle modifications, such as dietary changes and regular exercise, alongside pharmacological interventions like antihypertensive medications to reduce complications and improve long-term health outcomes.
Label: OpenAI

Text: Circadian rhythms are approximately twenty-four-hour biological cycles driven by molecular clocks in virtually every cell type. The core feedback loop involves CLOCK and BMAL1 proteins that activate transcription of Period and Cryptochrome genes, whose protein products then inhibit their own transcription, creating an oscillation. The suprachiasmatic nucleus coordinates peripheral clocks through neural and hormonal signals. Light entrains the clock through melanopsin-containing retinal ganglion cells projecting to the suprachiasmatic nucleus. Circadian disruption from shift work and jet lag is associated with increased risks of metabolic disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer. Clock genes regulate expression of many metabolic and physiological genes, explaining why time of day influences drug efficacy and toxicity.
Label: Anthropic

Text: Inorganic chemistry deals with synthesis and behavior of inorganic and organometallic compounds. This field covers chemical compounds that are not carbon-based, which are the subjects of organic chemistry. The distinction between the two disciplines is far from absolute, as there is much overlap in the subdiscipline of organometallic chemistry. It has applications in every aspect of the chemical industry, including catalysis, materials science, pigments, surfactants, coatings, medications, fuels, and agriculture. Many inorganic compounds are found in nature as minerals. Soil may contain iron sulfide as pyrite or calcium sulfate as gypsum.
Label: Human

Text: Bio-hybrid robots are advanced systems that integrate biological components, such as living cells or tissues, with synthetic robotic structures to create functional devices capable of performing complex tasks. These systems leverage the unique properties of biological materials, such as self-healing, adaptability, and energy efficiency, to enhance robotic performance. Typically, bio-hybrid robots incorporate muscle cells for actuation, neurons for control, or other biological elements to achieve specific functionalities. Applications of bio-hybrid robots span fields such as medicine, environmental monitoring, and bioengineering, offering potential solutions for challenges like targeted drug delivery, tissue repair, and pollutant detection. The development of bio-hybrid robots requires interdisciplinary collaboration, combining expertise in biology, robotics, materials science, and engineering to address challenges in integration, scalability, and ethical considerations.
Label: OpenAI

Text: Protein synthesis is a fundamental biological process by which cells construct proteins, essential macromolecules that perform a wide range of functions within organisms. This process occurs in two main stages: transcription and translation. During transcription, the DNA sequence of a gene is transcribed into messenger RNA (mRNA) within the nucleus. The mRNA then exits the nucleus and travels to the ribosome, where translation occurs. In translation, transfer RNA (tRNA) molecules deliver specific amino acids to the ribosome, guided by the codon sequence of the mRNA. The ribosome facilitates the formation of peptide bonds between amino acids, creating a polypeptide chain that folds into a functional protein. Protein synthesis is tightly regulated and critical for cellular growth, repair, and overall function.
Label: OpenAI

Text: Sound is a phenomenon in which pressure disturbances propagate through a transmission medium. In the context of physics, it is characterised as a mechanical wave of pressure or related quantities (e.g. displacement), whereas in physiological-psychological contexts it refers to the reception of such waves and their perception by the brain. Though sensitivity to sound varies among all organisms, the human ear is sensitive to frequencies ranging from 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Examples of the significance and application of sound include music, medical imaging techniques, oral language and parts of science.
Label: Human
